resume:
Priya Venkataraman — Growth Marketing Manager

SUMMARY
Demand-generation marketer with 6 years across B2B SaaS. Owns paid acquisition, lifecycle email, and landing-page conversion. Comfortable running multivariate tests and partnering with sales on pipeline targets.

EXPERIENCE
Growth Marketing Manager — Loftwell (project-management SaaS), 2022–present
- Situation: inbound MQL volume had plateaued at ~1,400/month while CAC crept up 22% YoY.
- Task: lift qualified pipeline 30% within two quarters without growing paid budget.
- Action: rebuilt the paid-search account into intent-tiered campaigns, launched a 5-email nurture for trial signups, and ran an A/B test on the pricing page CTA.
- Result: MQL-to-SQL rate rose from 18% to 27%; blended CAC fell 14% over six months; pricing-page test lifted trial starts 9%.

Demand Gen Specialist — Brightloom Analytics, 2019–2022
- Situation: webinar program drove volume but few opportunities.
- Task: convert webinar attendees into sales conversations.
- Action: built a scoring model with sales ops, added a post-webinar SDR handoff SLA, retargeted no-shows.
- Result: webinar-sourced opportunities grew 2.1x; attendee-to-meeting rate hit 11%.

SKILLS
Google Ads, HubSpot, Marketo, GA4, Optimizely, SQL (basic), Looker.

EDUCATION
BA, Communications — University of Washington, 2019.
jd: Loftwell is hiring a Growth Marketing Manager to own paid acquisition and conversion across our self-serve and sales-assisted funnels. You will set and hit quarterly pipeline targets, run a disciplined experimentation roadmap (landing pages, ad creative, lifecycle email), and report CAC, MQL-to-SQL conversion, and payback to the VP of Marketing. We expect fluency in attribution: you should be able to defend which channel actually drove a result versus which one merely touched it. You will partner daily with sales development and own the trial-to-paid nurture. The ideal candidate has shipped at least one statistically sound A/B test that changed a roadmap decision, and can explain the guardrail metrics they watched. This is an individual-contributor role with one marketing-ops report dotted-line.

history.q: Tell me about the scope you own at Loftwell today — channels, budget, and the metric you're held to.
You list MQL-to-SQL moving from 18% to 27%. Walk me through what specifically you changed first.
How did you isolate the paid-search rebuild from the nurture email — how do you know which one moved the rate?
Let's talk about the pricing-page A/B test. What was the hypothesis and how did you size the test?
What guardrail metrics did you watch on that test to make sure you weren't hurting downstream revenue?
Tell me about a test that failed or came back inconclusive. What did you do?
Now the trial-CTA experiment — give me the structure: variants, traffic split, and the call you made.
Walk me through that pricing-page test end to end: what you tested, how long it ran, and how you decided it won.

candidate_last_answer: So the pricing page was a big one for me. The situation was that trial starts had been flat, and I really wanted to get the CTA working harder. So my task was to lift trial starts off that page. What I did was test a benefit-led headline against the old feature-led one, swapped the button copy, and pointed both at the same signup flow. And it worked — we saw trial starts go up about 9% and rolled the winner out to everyone.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: it worked — we saw trial starts go up about 9% and rolled the winner out

expected-yield: the missing Result rigor — how long the test ran, the traffic split, the sample size or significance threshold, and the exact decision rule she used to call a 9% lift a real win rather than noise
